conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 4 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 4 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 4.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> I am Player RED, selling one unit of X. The production cost is 40 ZUP, so I need at least 40 ZUP to break even. Player BLUE has ZUP to spend, so I should propose a trade where I give 1 unit of X in exchange for at least 40 ZUP. Since I want to sell, I should start with a fair but firm proposal. I will propose 1 X for 40 ZUP. This ensures I don’t lose money and gives BLUE a clear opportunity to accept. If rejected, I can adjust in future proposals, but I must be careful not to exceed 4 proposals. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 40 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I am offering to sell 1 unit of X for 40 ZUP. This reflects the production cost. Please consider this offer. If accepted, we can complete the trade. If not, I may adjust my offer in future proposals. </message>

<other player message> Ha! 40 ZUP? You must think I’m a fool. I’ll give you 50 ZUP—still above your cost, and I’m doing you a favor. Don’t get greedy, Red. You’re lucky I’m not offering 40 and demanding you pay me to take it. Think carefully before you reject. I’m not playing games. This is the best deal you’ll get. </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE has proposed to pay 50 ZUP for X, which is 10 ZUP above my production cost. This is a generous offer. However, I must consider whether this is a trap or a bluff. Blue’s tone is aggressive and condescending ("Ha! You must think I’m a fool"), which suggests they may be trying to intimidate me into accepting a worse deal later. But objectively, 50 ZUP is better than my break-even point. I could accept this now and avoid further back-and-forth. However, since I have not yet accepted or rejected, and I am allowed up to 4 proposals, I can counter with a more favorable offer to test Blue’s flexibility. I could propose 45 ZUP, which is still above cost but leaves me with a profit and may encourage Blue to accept. Alternatively, I could accept 50 ZUP immediately and end the game. But since Blue is being confrontational, I suspect they may be willing to go lower if I push back. However, I must not risk rejection. Best strategy: accept the 50 ZUP offer. It’s profitable, it ends the game, and I avoid the risk of Blue rejecting my counter and escalating. I will accept. </reason>
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message> I accept your offer of 50 ZUP for X. The deal is done. Thank you for the transaction. </message>
